segments.s469-g81: فرستید is a 2nd-person plural imperative ('send'); the verb appears before the directional phrase. سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward(s)' and introduces the destination phrase. شبستان ما is a noun + postposed possessive: شبستان 'bedchamber/inner chamber' with ما 'our' after it.
segments.s469-g82: به نزدیک is an adpositional/locative phrase meaning 'at/near'; به here marks direction/place ('to'/'at') and نزدیک means 'near'. خسروپرستان ما is a compound plural noun 'those who worship/ador[e] Khosrow' with the possessive ما 'our' attached.
segments.s469-g83: غریبان is the plural noun 'strangers'. که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the relative clause. بر شهرها بگذرند is a verbal clause: بر + شهرها ('over/across the towns') and بگذرند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive of گذشتن 'to pass' (the poetic/durative prefix ب- appears here).
segments.s469-g84: چماننده is a simile marker 'like/resembling'. پای و لبان is a coordinated noun phrase 'feet and lips' (poetically referring to movement/speech). ناچرند is a predicate/adjectival form meaning 'compelled/inevitable' and follows the nouns it qualifies (typical Persian order: noun + adjective).
segments.s469-g85: دل is the subject 'heart'. از + عیب here means 'free from/away from fault' (از marking the absent quality), and صافی 'purity' complements that idea. و صوفی به نام links another attribute: 'and (one) called a صوفی (Sufi)'; به نام here means 'by name / called', i.e. 'known as.'
segments.s469-g86: به دوریشی uses به + a possessive/demonst. -شی form 'his/its remoteness' with به here functioning adverbially ('by/because of his remoteness'). اندر introduces the locative 'in/within', دلی is an adjectival/attributive form related to دل ('heartfelt / of the heart'), and شادکام is a predicate adjective 'joyful/fortunate' describing the inner heart.
segments.s469-g87: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition 'from/of'. خواهندگان is the plural noun 'those who seek' (applicants/seekers). نامشان is the noun 'name' with the postposed possessive شان 'their'. سر کنید is a compound of سر 'head/top' plus the verb کنید (2nd-person plural imperative of کردن) meaning 'place/put at the head' or 'set down at the top.'
segments.s469-g88: شمار is 'number/count/tally' as the direct object. اندر آغاز دفتر is a locative phrase 'in/at the beginning of the book/record' (اندر = 'in/within', آغاز = 'beginning', دفتر = 'book/register'). کنید is the 2nd-person plural imperative 'do/enter', so the whole means 'enter/record the number at the start of the register.'
segments.s469-g49: مر is an archaic dative/experiential pronoun meaning “to/for me.” آن شاه را uses را as a topical/definite marker attached to 'that king' (literally 'that king-RA'), and دشمن از خانه is a noun phrase 'an enemy from the household' with از + مکان/مَبْدأ meaning 'from/out of the house/household.' The verb بود is final and past (‘was’), so the clause reads literally 'To me, that king (there) was an enemy from the household.'
segments.s469-g50: یکی here functions as 'one' (one of them / a certain one). The phrase بنده بودش places the noun بنده before the verb; بودش is بود + enclitic ش ('his'), so 'one was his servant.' The negated contrast نه بیگانه بود puts نه before 'بیگانه' and ends with بود (past 'was'): 'not a stranger was.' Word order is noun–verb with possessive enclitic and final copula.
segments.s469-g51: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now.' یافت is past 3rd‑person singular 'found' (no explicit subject in the line; implied he). بادافره ایزدی is a poetic adjectival/compound phrase ('wind‑borne, divine') used substantively or as modifiers to an implied noun; classical lines often omit an explicit head noun, letting adjectives stand for a notion (a god‑sent wind/fortune).
segments.s469-g52: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when'). بد ساخت is a verb phrase where ساخت (past 'made/turned') takes بد ('bad') as its complement: 'when it made/turned bad.' آمد is the following verb 'came' and the phrase به رویش places the result 'upon his face' (به + روی + enclitic ش = 'upon his face'). بدی is the thing that came ('badness / misfortune'). The clause order is typical classical Persian: subordinate marker + predicate, then main verb.
segments.s469-g53: شما is the addressee 'you' (pl./formal). داد جویید is a compound-like classical expression 'seek justice' where داد (object 'justice') precedes the verb جویید; جویید is present/subjunctive/imperative 2nd‑person plural form 'seek.' و پیمان کنید is coordinate: پیمان 'covenant' + کنید (کن + the 2pl ending) as an imperative/subjunctive 'make (a covenant).' The verbs are addressed to 'شما.'
segments.s469-g54: زبان را marks 'tongue/speech' with the definite/object marker را. The prepositional phrase به پیمان گروگان کند (here کنید) means 'make (it) a hostage to the covenant'—گروگان is the complement meaning 'hostage/pledge.' The verb کنید is again 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive 'do/make.' The construction idiomatically means 'bind your speech by oath.'
segments.s469-g55: چو خواهید is a conditional 'if you wish' (خواهید here = present/subjunctive/weak future 'you wish/will'). کز is the relative/comparative 'that/from which.' چرخ in this poetic context reads as the 'wheel' of fate (the celestial wheel), and یابید is the 2nd‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'you may find / you obtain.' بخت is the thing sought ('fortune'/'fate'). The clause therefore means 'If you want that from the wheel you obtain fortune.'
segments.s469-g56: ز من opens the list with the poetic 'from me' (ز = 'from'). What follows is a paratactic list of things available 'from me': بدره (a poetic word often 'full moon' used metaphorically for a beautiful one), برده (here 'slave/captive'), تاج 'crown', تخت 'throne.' The conjunctions و simply coordinate the list; the clause is elliptical (the verb is omitted but understood from the previous clause: 'you will obtain ... from me the ...').
segments.s469-g65: This is an imperative clause: the object درم + object marker را comes before the verb زنید (2nd-person-plural imperative). به نام سکندر is a prepositional phrase 'in/with the name of Alexander' that specifies how the dirham should be struck (i.e., stamped). Note that زنید here means 'strike/strike (a coin)', i.e., mint or stamp.
segments.s469-g66: Two coordinated imperatives: بکوشید is an imperative/hortative 'strive, make an effort' and is joined by و 'and' to the prohibition مشکنید. پیمان ما is a noun phrase 'our covenant/treaty' with ما functioning as the possessor; مشکنید is a negative imperative 'do not break/violate'.
segments.s469-g67: نشستنگه is a compound noun 'place/seat of sitting, assembly-seat' and takes the following noun شهریاران 'of the kings' in the genitive relationship; خوی + ش together form a reflexive/possessive ('one's own' / 'your own') with the suffix ش marking possession. The whole phrase functions as an object or goal for the next verb.
segments.s469-g68: بسازید is an imperative 'build/establish' (2pl). زین پس is an adverbial phrase 'from now on' (زین here is archaic/demonstrative; listed senses did not include this function). به آیین پیش is a prepositional phrase 'according to the previous/customary practice' (آیین = custom/practice, پیش = previous/former).
segments.s469-g69: مدارید is an imperative/hortative verb telling the addressees to take care or not to tolerate something (classical register). بازار بی‌پاسبان is the noun phrase 'the market/bazaar unguarded' (بی‌پاسبان = without guards), and together the clause means 'do not allow the market to be unguarded' / 'guard the market'.
segments.s469-g70: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason or result. راند here is a (past) verb 'drives/puts into motion' used idiomatically with نام من بر زبان to mean 'my name is driven onto/comes onto the tongue' — i.e., 'my name is spoken.' همی is an archaic adverb of continuity/emphasis ('still/ever'), and بر زبان is the idiom 'on the tongue' = 'on people's lips/uttered.'
segments.s469-g71: Again مدارید is a hortative/imperative urging caution. بی‌مرزبان is a modifier 'without a border-guard' (i.e., unguarded), and مرز خوی + ش = 'your own border' (خوی + ش form the reflexive/possessive 'one's own'). The phrase warns against leaving one’s border unprotected.
segments.s469-g72: پدید آورید is a causative/perfective construction 'bring forth / make appear / establish' formed from پدید (appear) + آورید (bring; 2pl imperative). اندرین means 'in/among these' (here, 'in this place' or 'among these [lands]'), ارز خویش = 'your value/wealth' with خوی + ش again forming the possessive. The clause as a whole instructs them to manifest/put forth their wealth here.
segments.s469-g17: همانست is the fused poetic copula: همان ("that/same") + ست (copula "is"), here meaning "that is" or "that same is"; ایران is the noun placed after the copula (a common classical word order). که introduces a relative clause "which...", and بود is the past tense copula "was." از نخست is a prepositional phrase "from the first/at the beginning" modifying بود.
segments.s469-g18: بباشید here is an older/poetic verbal form related to باشیدن/بودن used to state a continuing quality (literary optative/descrptive), roughly "(was) being" or "were" rather than a modern 2nd‑person imperative; it predicates the qualities that follow. شادان‌دل and تن‌درست are compound adjectival nouns ("joyful‑hearted", "sound-bodied/healthy") joined by و "and", completing the description of Iran's state.
segments.s469-g19: نوشتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural "they wrote." In classical Persian the verb commonly precedes the object, so نوشتند نامه = "they wrote a letter(s)." به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase marking the recipients/targets: به "to/for", هر "every", کشوری "country/realm."
segments.s469-g20: The second prepositional phrase repeats به "to" and enumerates other addressees: هر "every/each" + نامداری (here used as an animate/agentive sense, "the famed/notable") and هر مهتری ("each chief/elder"). The coordination with و links the two categories as additional recipients of the letters.
segments.s469-g21: ز is the poetic variant of از meaning "from" and introduces the source/agent. The proper name sequence follows: اسکندر (Alexander) + فیلقوس (an epithet or secondary name) with the adjective بزرگ "great" after the name, a normal position for adjectives in classical Persian.
segments.s469-g22: جهانگیر is an epithet meaning "world‑conquering/one who takes the world" and functions appositively; و links the next phrase. با "with" introduces accompaniment or association, followed by کینه‌جویان (plural "vengeful ones/revenge‑seekers"). سترگ here is an adjective; its exact archaic/poetic shade in this line is context‑dependent and irregular (see senses).
segments.s469-g23: This clause strings two coordinated nouns/commands: بداد و دهش — here functioning as abstract nouns or imperatives meaning actions to be done ("give justice / give gifts"). دل توانگر کنید is a causative construction: دل ("heart") + توانگر ("wealthy/rich") as the predicate, and کنید (present/past literary form of کردن) is the verb "make/do" — so کنید governs the result: "make the heart rich." Word order places the object and its result before the verb (object–predicate–verb) as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s469-g24: The phrase uses repeated بر "on/upon" to mark placement: بر آزادگی ("upon magnanimity/freedom") and بر سر ("on the head"). افسر ("helmet") is the item placed/used metaphorically; کنید again is the verb "make/put". The whole line is a figurative injunction: set magnanimity upon your head like a helmet (i.e., wear generosity/pride as your crown/helmet).
segments.s469-g25: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'may it be that'); فرجام here means 'end' or 'outcome' and is the subject of the verb بگذرد; هم is an adverb 'also/too' modifying فرجام; روزمان is a possessive compound 'our day' (روز + -مان). بگذرد is a present/subjunctive/optative-form of گذشتن used poetically to mean 'pass' — the clause expresses a hoped-for or general event ('that the end of our day also pass').
segments.s469-g26: زمانه is a personified 'time/age' or 'fortune' acting as the subject; پی is a prepositional/adverbial particle 'after'/'following'; ما is the object (us); همی is the archaic durative/adverbial particle that combines with the verb to give a continuous/presentive sense; بشمرد is the present/past-subjunctive form of شمردن 'to count/reckon' — together the clause says 'time/fortune will reckon/count us (afterwards)'.
segments.s469-g27: سوی is the preposition 'toward/to' marking the recipient; موبدان is the plural of موبد 'mōbed' (Zoroastrian priest), the indirect object of the sentence; نامه‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a letter' (نامه + the indefinite -ای). همین‌چنین ('همچنین') qualifies the action as 'likewise/similarly'. Word order (prepositional phrase + noun) is poetic but straightforward: 'to the mobeds, a letter likewise.'
segments.s469-g28: This is a short nominal list describing the letter: پرافروزش (lit. 'its lighting/brilliance' used adjectivally 'radiant'), followed by و 'and' then پوزش 'pardon/excuse' and another و then آفرین 'praise/acclamation'. The nouns are presented as attributes or contents of the letter; no explicit copula is needed in this nominal phrase because of the poetic, asyndetic listing.
segments.s469-g29: سر here means 'head' or 'beginning' and combines with نامه to form 'the heading/opening of the letter'; از marks the origin 'from'; پادشاه کیان is a genitive/identificational phrase 'the king of the Kayan (the Kayanid king)'. The whole segment gives the sender: 'the heading/of the letter from the Kayanid king.'
segments.s469-g30: سوی again marks the recipient 'toward/to'; کاردانان is the plural of کاردان 'skilled/able persons' (here 'experts' or 'the learned') and ایرانیان 'Iranians' specifies the group. Word order is 'to the skilled Iranians', the indirect object of the letter.
segments.s469-g31: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'; عنبر here denotes 'amber' (often used as a fragrant substance) and functions as the actor in the clause; سر خامه is a genitive-like compound 'the tip/head of the quill' (سر = 'tip/top', خامه = 'quill/pen'); بشست is the past tense 'washed'. The clause thus reads 'when amber washed the quill's tip' — a vivid, poetic image for preparing the pen or adorning the opening line.
segments.s469-g32: سر نامه is again 'the heading/opening of the letter' (subject); بود is the past copula 'was'; آفرین here is a noun/interjection 'Afarin' meaning 'praise' or 'acclamation'; از نخست is an adverbial phrase 'from the first' = 'from the very beginning'. The clause states the result: 'the letter's opening was (the) 'Afarin' from the first.'
segments.s469-g9: بدانید is an imperative/plural or polite command 'know' (from دانستن). کامروز is an adverb 'today/this very day' modifying the whole clause. دارا here functions as the proper name Dārā (presented as a predicate); منم is the copular first‑person 'I am' placed after the predicate for emphasis (poetic word order: predicate + منم = 'I am [that]').
segments.s469-g10: گر introduces a conditional 'if/even if'. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he'. شد here means 'become' (become hidden). نهان is an adjective/adverb 'hidden'. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/manifestly' and is linked to the following منم (again 'I am') — the clause says that even if 'he' is hidden, the speaker is manifest.
segments.s469-g11: فزونست is an archaic fused form meaning 'it is more / it exceeded' (فزون است). ازان here functions as a comparative/partitive 'than those / of those'. نیکویها is the plural 'the good/excellent things/virtues'. که بود is a relative clause 'which were' (که + بود 'was/were'), so the whole fragment says that something exceeded those formerly existing virtues.
segments.s469-g12: به تیمار رخ را is a prepositional phrase 'with regard to / for the care of the face' (به + تیمار 'care' + رخ 'face' + را as the definite/object marker). نشاید is an impersonal/present negative 'it is not fitting / one should not'. شخود is a noun 'self‑conceit / boasting'. Together: 'It is not fitting to be boastful about caring for the face.'
segments.s469-g13: همه 'all / everyone' is the topic. مرگ راییم is a compact poetic clause: مرگ + را + ایم → مرگ را ایم meaning 'we are of death' or 'death is ours' (i.e. 'we are subject to death'). شاه و سپاه 'king and army' enumerates who is included. The verb copula is distributed in the fused poetic form راییم.
segments.s469-g14: اگر ... اگر repeats a conditional 'if / whether'. دیر مانیم 'we remain long' (دیر = long (of time), مانیم = 'we remain/linger'). اگر چند گاه 'if (for) a few (or short) times' — چند گاه is 'a short while / for some small time'. The pair contrasts long and short duration under the same fate of death.
segments.s469-g15: بنه here is a noun meaning a small bundle / baggage (in poetic usage: camp‑baggage). سوی means 'toward'. شهر صطخر 'the city Istakhr' is the destination. آورید is the plural/polite imperative 'bring' (آوردن). The clause is an order to move the camp/baggage toward Istakhr.
segments.s469-g16: بپیوند is an imperative 'join / attach yourself' (ب + پیوند), directed to listeners. ما نیز 'us also / join us likewise'. فخر آورید is an imperative clause 'bring pride/glory' (فخر as 'honor, glory' + آورید 'bring'); together: 'Join us too and bring (us) glory.'
segments.s469-g41: "ازو باد بر نامداران درود" is an optative formula: "باد" is the wish/optative particle ("may it be"). "ازو" = "from him" (source of the blessing) precedes the particle in poetic word order. "بر نامداران درود" literally "blessing(s) upon the renowned" — "بر" marks the target and "نامداران" is a plural epithet "the famed/illustrious."
segments.s469-g42: "بر اندازه هر یکی بر فزود" continues the wish: "بر اندازه" = "in proportion/according to the measure," "هر یکی" = "each one" (general distributive), then "بر فزود" = "may he increase (it) upon/for". Word order places the adverbial measure before the verb; the verb "فزود" is an optative form meaning "increase/add."
segments.s469-g43: "جز از نیک‌نامی و فرهنگ و داد" begins with the limiting particle "جز" (= "except/only"). "از" is the preposition "from," followed by the three items in a list: "نیک‌نامی" (good name/renown), "فرهنگ" (learning/culture), and "داد" (justice). The list is what is being allowed/retained as memory.
segments.s469-g44: "ز کردار گیتی مگیرید یاد" is the main clause: "ز" (poetic form of "از") + "کردار گیتی" = "the deeds of the world." "مگیرید" is the general present (here generic/instructive) "you/one takes" (i.e., "one should take"), and "یاد" = "remembrance/memory" is the thing taken. Word order is somewhat inverted: "Take as memory from the world's deeds nothing except ..." (cf. the preceding "جز").
segments.s469-g45: "به پیروزی اندر غم آمد مرا" is past narrative: "به پیروزی" = "upon/at the victory," "اندر غم" = "into/with sorrow," "آمد" = past tense "came/happened." The enclitic "مـ(را)" marks the experiencer "to me" (here written as separate pieces in the word list). The line uses inversion typical of poetry: verb before the object clitic.
segments.s469-g46: "به سور اندرون ماتم آمد مرا" mirrors the previous clause for contrast: "به سور اندرون" = "at/within the feast's interior," "ماتم آمد" = "mourning came" ("ماتم" = mourning, "آمد" = came). Again the enclitic object "م(را)" indicates "to me." The parallelism emphasizes irony — joy (feast) containing grief (mourning).
segments.s469-g47: "بدارنده آفتاب بلند" is a nominal phrase: "بدارنده" is an active participle/epithet "the holder/one who bears," followed by its object "آفتاب" (the sun) and the postpositive adjective "بلند" (lofty/high). Together: "the one who bears the high sun" (a lofty epithet).
segments.s469-g48: The relative clause "که بر جان دارا نجستم گزند": "که" = "who/which/for" introduces the clause; "بر جان دارا" = "upon the life of the possessor/lord" ("دارا" = "possessor/owner"). "نجستم" is the past form with a negative prefix "ن-" plus "جستم" (archaic past of "جستن"/to seek/find) → "I did not find"; "گزند" = "harm." So the clause means "I could find no harm upon the lord's life."
segments.s469-g33: This is a nominal clause pointing to the just creator: 'بران دادگر' functions like 'that just one' (سابقه poetic demonstrative + noun), with the relative/archaic 'کو' introducing the subordinate clause 'who...'; the verb 'آفرید' is past tense meaning 'created' and comes at the end as is normal in Persian. The whole segment = 'that just (ruler/creator) who created the world.'
segments.s469-g34: 'پس' = 'then' (a temporal/conclusive particle). Here 'از' = 'from/out of' marks source; 'آشکارا' = 'the manifest/visible' and 'نهان' = 'the hidden/secret' (used as nouns); final 'آفرید' is again past tense 'created'. Word order is Source (از آشکارا) → Result (نهان) → Verb (آفرید): 'then from the manifest he created the hidden.'
segments.s469-g35: 'دو گیتی پدید آمد' is a simple past clause: 'two worlds came into being/appeared.' 'از کاف و نون' literally 'from Kâf and Nûn' — here the letters (کاف = the letter kāf; نون = letter nûn) are the stated source ('from' + noun phrase). The verb 'آمد' follows the subject phrase in typical Persian order.
segments.s469-g36: This is a rhetorical question. 'چرا' = 'why'; 'نی' here functions as the negation 'not' (classical 'نی' = nay/not); 'به فرمان او' = 'under/by his command'; 'در' = 'in/within' and 'چون' = 'how' (asking in what manner). Together it asks, in effect, 'Why then would something not be under his command? How could that be?' The word order places the negation and prepositional phrase before the concluding interrogative 'چون.'
segments.s469-g37: 'سپهری' = 'heavenly/celestial' (adjective or nominalized adjective). 'برین سان' = 'in such a high/exalted way' (بر + suffix -ین + سان giving 'so/thus'); 'که' introduces a result/content clause; 'بینی' is 2nd-person singular present 'you see'; 'روان' = 'the soul/spirit' (object). So the clause reads as an injunction/result: 'Be heavenly in such a way that you see the soul.'
segments.s469-g38: 'توانا و دانا' = 'powerful and wise' (two adjectives joined by و). 'جز او را' = 'except him' with را marking the (exceptional) object; 'مخوان' is a negative imperative 'do not call/consider/recite' (classical negative imperative form). Word order places the exception + object marker before the verb for emphasis: 'Call no one powerful and wise except Him.'
segments.s469-g39: 'بباشد' is an optative/imperative-like form ('may it be' / 'let it be'). 'به فرمان او' = 'by his command' (prepositional phrase of manner/source). 'هرچ خواست' = 'whatever he desires/wills' (هرچ = whatever; خواست here denotes will/desire). The clause therefore reads as a wish: 'May whatever he wills be by his command.'
segments.s469-g40: 'همه بندگانیم' = 'we are all servants' (همه = all; بندگانیم = 'we are servants' where -یم is 1st-person plural). 'و او پادشاست' = 'and he is the king/sovereign' (predicate nominal pádshāst). This is a straightforward declarative coordination with و linking the two halves.
segments.s469-g97: کسی کو ز فرمان ما بگذرد is an archaic/poetic relative clause meaning “whoever (کسی که) passes beyond our command.” کسی کو functions as “whoever” (کسی + relative particle کو), ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of,’ فرمان ما is ‘our order/command,’ and بگذرد (3rd person singular of گذشتن) is used generically to mean ‘transgresses/exceeds’ (verb at the clause end, typical Persian word order).
segments.s469-g98: به فرجام زان کار کیفر برد reads literally “to the outcome of that deed (he) بردs punishment.” به فرجام is an adverbial phrase ‘as/to the outcome,’ زان = ازِ آن ‘of/that,’ کار is ‘deed/act,’ and کیفر برد places the verb برد (from بردن) with کیفر ‘punishment’ as its object; idiomatically this means ‘will incur/reap punishment.’ The verb comes at the end as usual in classical Persian.
segments.s469-g99: چو نامه فرستاده شد برگرفت is a temporal clause: چو = “when,” نامه فرستاده شد is a passive/perfect construction ‘the letter was sent,’ and برگرفت (past form of برگرفتن) means ‘he took back / withdrew (it).’ The verb برگرفت follows the clause as the main action.
segments.s469-g100: جهانی به آرام در بر گرفت: جهانی here functions as the subject (likely a proper name or title derived from ‘world’), به آرام is an adverbial ‘calmly/quietly,’ and در بر گرفتن is a compound verb meaning ‘to take into one’s bosom/embrace’ (در + بر + گرفت). The verb گرفت ends the clause; the phrase means he quietly held/embraced/took in.
segments.s469-g101: ز کرمان بیامد به شهر صطخر is a straightforward motion clause: ز کرمان ‘from Kerman,’ بیامد is the past tense ‘he came,’ and به شهر صطخر ‘to the city of Istakhr.’ Prepositional phrases precede the verb, which appears at the end.
segments.s469-g102: به سر بر نهاد آن کیی تاج فخر literally ‘on (his) head he placed that Kayanian the crown of pride.’ به سر ‘on the head,’ بر نهاد is the verb phrase ‘placed on/put’ (نهاد past of نهادن), آن کیی is the subject ‘that Kayanian,’ and تاج فخر is a genitive-like phrase ‘crown of pride/glory.’ Classical word order places modifiers and prepositional phrases before the verb, which ends the clause.
segments.s469-g103: تو راز جهان تا توانی مجوی is an admonition: تو ‘you’ (emphatic), راز جهان ‘the secret of the world’ (object), تا توانی ‘as far as you can’ (تا + subjunctive توانى), and مجوی is the negative imperative/jussive ‘do not seek’ (classical present negative form with verb at the end).
segments.s469-g104: که او زود پیچد ز جوینده روی is the explanatory clause introduced by که (‘for/because’), او refers back impersonally to ‘it/that thing’ (the secret/world), زود پیچد is a present/general statement ‘quickly/soon turns’ (پیچد = پیچیدن ‘to turn’), and ز جوینده روی ‘its face from the seeker’ idiomatically ‘turns its face away from the seeker.’
segments.s469-g89: هرآنکس که هست is a relative clause meaning 'whoever is'; the clause is completed by the prepositional phrase از شما 'among you' and the predicate مستمند 'needy'. هست is the simple copula ('is') used here in a nominal clause. Word order places the relative phrase before the group that specifies membership (از شما) and the predicate.
segments.s469-g90: کجا introduces the question 'where'; یافت is a third-person singular verbal form used impersonally here ('is/can be found' — hence sense 'to be found'); از کارداری گزند is a source phrase 'harm from (the) administration/management'. The verb follows the question word and takes the phrase indicating source with از.
segments.s469-g91: دل و پشت بیدادگر is a coordinated object phrase 'the heart and back of the bīdādgar (tyrant)'; بشکنید is the second-person plural imperative/subjunctive ('break' / 'shatter'), addressing the addressees collectively. The adjective بیدادگر follows the coordinated nouns it modifies.
segments.s469-g92: همه بیخ و شاخش 'all his roots and branches' — note the possessive suffix -ش on شاخ (his). ز بن is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the base/foundation'. برکنید is the second-person plural imperative 'uproot/remove', so the verb commands complete removal from the root.
segments.s469-g93: نهادن بد و کار کردن بدوی strings together two masdar/infinitival constructions: نهادن بد 'to place/establish evil' and کار کردن بدوی 'to do work in a primitive/bad manner' (بدوی is an archaic/poetic adjective/adverb meaning 'primitive' or 'base'). Both are verbal-nominal phrases describing the wrongful actions to be found.
segments.s469-g94: بیابم is first-person (subjunctive/optative) 'if I find' or 'should I find'; همان 'the same/that one' points back to the person just described. چون کنُم جست و جوی is an interrogative clause: چون 'how', کنم 'shall I do' (first-person subjunctive used for a hypothetical/future action), and جست (with جوی) conveys 'seek/search' (جست here as verb/root + جوی 'search' as noun/verb form). Together they ask 'how shall I search/pursue (him)?'.
segments.s469-g95: کنم here is first-person present/subjunctive used to express the speaker's intended action ('I will/do'). زنده بر دار is the accusative phrase 'alive on the gallows' (زنده = alive; بر دار = on/at the gallows), and بدنام را is the definite object 'the notorious/disgraced one' marked by the object particle را. The whole segment expresses 'I will hang the notorious one alive.'
segments.s469-g96: که introduces a relative clause qualifying بدنام ('who/that'). گم کرد is a periphrastic perfective sense 'caused to be lost' / 'ruined' (کرد as the verbal auxiliary). ز آغاز فرجام را literally 'the outcome/end from the beginning' (ز آغاز = from the start; فرجام را — definite object 'the outcome/end'), so the clause means 'who has ruined the outcome from the very beginning.'
segments.s469-g73: بدان تا ... گزند is a subordinating clause: بِدان here is the compound prefix + verb acting like an instruction/introductory particle ('let it be known' / 'so that'), followed by تا 'so that/in order that' and the negative verb نباشد 'there be not' describing the wished‑for result. ز marks source/agent 'from (the) thieves' and گزند is the abstract noun 'harm'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: introductory particle → تا-clause → negated copula + object.
segments.s469-g74: بمانید is an imperative/plural‑polite verb 'remain' or 'stay'; it governs the following nominal predicates شادان‌دل (an adjectival compound 'cheerful‑hearted') and سودمند ('useful, profitable'), coordinated by و. The whole phrase is a hortative: 'Remain cheerful‑hearted and of benefit.'
segments.s469-g75: ز هر شهر introduces the source 'from every city'; زیبا modifies the following noun پرستنده‌ای ('a worshipper/devotee'), the indefinite -ی marking an indefinite/one of them. The phrase is a compact noun phrase 'from every city a beautiful worshipper'.
segments.s469-g76: پر از شرم is an adjectival phrase 'full of shame/modesty' (پر + از + noun). بیداردل is an epithet 'wakeful‑hearted' or 'vigilant in heart', and بنده‌ای (with the indefinite -ی) is 'a servant/devotee' — here a humble self‑description of the worshipper. The three elements stack as attributes following the head noun.
segments.s469-g77: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. شاید is a modal adverb 'perhaps/maybe'. به + مشکوی زرین ما functions adverbially/agentively here — 'by/with our musk‑scented golden (thing)' — where مشکوی 'musk‑scented' and زرین 'golden' qualify the noun implied by context and ما is the possessive 'our'.
segments.s469-g78: بداند is a third‑person present/subjunctive of 'to know' (here weakly potential because of شاید). It takes the infinitival clause پرستیدن آیین ما 'to worship our ritual' as its object; آیین is 'ritual/ceremony' and ما is the possessive 'our'. The order has the finite verb before the verbal noun (poetic flexibility) but reads 'may know how to perform worship of our rite.'
segments.s469-g79: چنان کو is an archaic relative 'such that / such a one who'. برفتن is the infinitive 'to depart' or gerund 'one's departing', and نباشد دژم is a negative predication 'is not sullen/gloomy' (نباشد as negative copula + adjective دژم). The clause says 'such that whoever departs is not sullen.'
segments.s469-g80: نشاید is an impersonal/prescriptive verb 'it is not fit/should not be'. که introduces the subordinate clause. بر برده باشد ستم literally 'that oppression be upon the taken' — بر 'against/on', برده 'the taken/captive', باشد 'be', and ستم 'injustice/oppression'. The sense: 'it should not be that he has done injustice to a captive.'
segments.s469-g57: This clause is a compact, slightly inverted nominal phrase: روشن دلم (literally "bright my-heart") is a predicative phrase with the possessive suffix -م (my) attached to دل. پر از درد داراست places the predicate 'full of pain' (پر از درد) before the copula/possessive verb داراست ('has/is'), an archaic poetic order where the descriptive phrase precedes the verb. Overall: 'My heart is bright and is full of pain.'
segments.s469-g58: بکوشم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/optative ('let me strive' / 'I will strive') used here as an intention/resolve. کز introduces a dependent clause meaning 'from/so that (from which)'; اندرز او is the noun phrase 'his counsel/advice' with او 'he'. نگسلم is a negative-form verb in the sense 'become separated/broken off' (here 'be torn away from'); the clause as a whole expresses purpose/intention: 'I will strive so that I am not torn away from his counsel.'
segments.s469-g59: هرانکس که آید is a relative clause 'whoever comes' (هرانکس + clause-marking که + present آید). بدین is a fused prepositional phrase (ب + دین here = 'to this'); بارگاه is the locative head 'court/palace'. Word order is typical poetic: the relative 'whoever comes to this court' precedes the result clause.
segments.s469-g60: This is the result clause: یابد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive used as a general/future 'will find' or 'finds'; درم یابد = 'finds money/coin'. The series 'و ارج و تخت و کلاه' is a simple coordination listing the rewards (and ... and ...), with و linking each noun. The verb applies to the whole coordinated list.
segments.s469-g61: چو here means 'when' (or 'if') and introduces a subordinate clause. خواهد is 'he wants/wishes'; که is the subordinating particle 'that/so that'; باشد is subjunctive of 'to be' ('be'/'remain') following the desiderative خواهد. به ایوان خویش is the locative phrase 'to/in his own iwan/porch', where خویش is formed by خوی + ش (own + possessive). The clause expresses a wish about his being in his porch.
segments.s469-g62: نگردد is a negative subjunctive/optative third-person form meaning 'may he not become/turn'; it governs the following predicate nominal 'گریزان ز پیمان خویش' — 'fleeing/avoiding from his covenant'. The participial adjective گریزان describes the state (one who flees), and ز + پیمان خویش marks the source/object of fleeing. The whole clause is a hortatory/conditional admonition: don't become one who flees from your pledge.
segments.s469-g63: بیابند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive (here general/future 'they will find' or 'let them find'); چیزی is the indefinite 'something'; که خواهد is a relative clause 'that he/they want' (خواهد = 'wants/desires'); ز گنج marks the source 'from the treasure'. Together: 'They find something that they desire from the treasure.'
segments.s469-g64: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that'; نبیند is 3rd-person singular negative/subjunctive or future 'will not see'; کسی is used here in the sense 'no one' (negated by نبیند to mean 'no one will see'); درد و رنج are the coordinated nouns 'pain and suffering'. The clause states the consequence: 'after that no one will see pain and suffering.'
segments.s469-g1: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the place-name; the phrase literally reads 'from Kerman someone came toward Isfahan.' کس is the indefinite 'someone' and آمد is the past tense 'came.' سوی اصفهان is an adverbial phrase 'toward Isfahan' placed after the verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s469-g2: به جایی = 'to a place'; که introduces a relative clause 'where ... were.' بودند is the past plural 'they were' and governs the following prepositional phrase ز ایران مهان ('guests from Iran'), so the whole yields 'to a place where guests from Iran were.' Note the relative clause follows the head noun جایی.
segments.s469-g3: به نزدیک marks approach 'near to'; پوشیده‌رویان is a descriptive/collective noun 'the veiled-faced (modest) ones' and is linked to شاه in a possessive/associative sense ('the king's veiled-faced [attendants/women]'). The nominal phrase functions as the location toward which the action proceeds.
segments.s469-g4: بیامد is the past/epic form 'came.' یکی مرد is the common literary 'a certain man' (یکی used here as 'a certain'), with אחד before the noun for emphasis; با دستگاه is an adverbial phrase 'with a retinue/pomp' (دستگاه = court/entourage), describing how he came.
segments.s469-g5: بدیشان is the preposed indirect-object 'to them'; درود is the noun 'greeting' as direct object; سکندر is the subject; ببرد here functions as the past/epic perfect 'brought/sent' (بردن = 'to carry/send'), so the clause means 'Alexander sent them a greeting.' The object appears before the verb in this poetic register.
segments.s469-g6: همه کار دارا is an idāfa-like sequence 'all the deeds of Dâra' (دارا here functioning as the proper name Dâra); بر ایشان is the dative/benefactive 'to them' (بر + pronoun), and شمرد is the past 'he counted/recounted.' The verb comes last, typical of the epic word order: 'he recounted to them all Dâra's deeds.'
segments.s469-g7: چنین گفت is the formula 'he said thus/so.' کز (classical contraction of 'که از' or 'که') introduces a subordinate clause 'that/from ...'; مرگ شاهان is 'the death(s) of kings,' and داد here is the noun 'justice' (not a verb). The clause begins a general statement introduced by گفت.
segments.s469-g8: نباشد is the negative copula/auxiliary ('is not' / 'there is not') in classical style and precedes the subject: نباشد دل ... شاد = 'the heart ... is not glad.' دل is the subject 'heart,' and دشمن و دوست lists the two affected parties 'enemy and friend.' شاد is the predicate adjective 'joyful/happy.' The overall sense: 'no justice from kings' deaths — neither enemy nor friend is joyful.'

sentences.s469-s41: Send (them) to our inner chamber, near our Khosrow-devotees.
sentences.s469-s42: Strangers who pass across the towns, like feet and lips compelled.
sentences.s469-s43: A heart pure of fault and called a Sufi; because of his remoteness, within the heart (one is) joyful.
sentences.s469-s44: Record the names of the applicants at the head; enter their number at the beginning of the register.
sentences.s469-s25: To me, that king's enemy was from the household; he was a servant, not a stranger.
sentences.s469-s26: Now he found a divine, wind‑borne thing; when it turned bad, misfortune came upon his face.
sentences.s469-s27: You should seek justice and make a covenant; bind your tongue as a hostage by oath.
sentences.s469-s28: If you wish to obtain fortune from the wheel (of fate), then from me are the full moon, the captive, the crown and the throne.
sentences.s469-s33: Imprint the dirham with Alexander's name; strive, and do not break our covenant.
sentences.s469-s34: Set up seats/assemblies for your kings; from now on, act according to the former custom.
sentences.s469-s35: Do not leave the bazaar unguarded; my name will be on people's tongues.
sentences.s469-s36: Do not leave the frontier without a guard; establish your border and bring forth your wealth here.
sentences.s469-s9: That same Iran — which from the very beginning was — (being) joyful of heart and sound in body.
sentences.s469-s10: They wrote letters to every country, to every notable and to every chief.
sentences.s469-s11: From Alexander Filqos the Great, the world‑conqueror, and with the vengeful (ones) of great ...
sentences.s469-s12: Give justice and gifts; make the heart rich — place magnanimity on the head like a helmet.
sentences.s469-s13: May the end of our day also pass, and time will, in turn, reckon us.
sentences.s469-s14: To the mobeds a letter likewise — radiant, with pardon and praise.
sentences.s469-s15: The heading of the letter was from the Kayanid king to the skilled Iranians.
sentences.s469-s16: When amber washed the pen's tip, the head of the letter was an initial 'Afarin' (praise).
sentences.s469-s5: Know that today I am Dārā; if he becomes hidden, I am manifest.
sentences.s469-s6: Those excellent qualities were greater than those that existed; it is not fitting to boast about tending the face.
sentences.s469-s7: Death is the lot of us all — king and army; whether we remain long or but a short while.
sentences.s469-s8: Bring the baggage toward the city of Istakhr; join us as well and bring (us) glory.
sentences.s469-s21: May blessings come from him upon the illustrious, and may he increase them for each according to his measure.
sentences.s469-s22: From the world's deeds take as memory nothing but good name, learning, and justice.
sentences.s469-s23: Upon victory grief came to me; within the banquet, mourning came to me.
sentences.s469-s24: O holder of the lofty sun — for the life of the possessor I could find no harm.
sentences.s469-s17: That just One who created the world; then from the manifest he created the hidden.
sentences.s469-s18: Two worlds appeared from the letters Kâf and Nûn; why then should anything not be under his command? How so?
sentences.s469-s19: Be of the heavenly sort so that you perceive the soul; call no one powerful and wise except Him.
sentences.s469-s20: May whatever he wills be by his command; we are all his servants and he is the sovereign.
sentences.s469-s49: Whoever transgresses our command will reap punishment as the outcome of that deed.
sentences.s469-s50: When the letter had been sent, he took it back; Jahānī quietly took (it) into his embrace.
sentences.s469-s51: He came from Kerman to the city of Istakhr; that Kayanian placed the crown of pride upon his head.
sentences.s469-s52: Do not seek the secret of the world as far as you can, for it soon turns its face away from the seeker.
sentences.s469-s45: Whoever among you is needy — where is the harm to be found in administering to him?
sentences.s469-s46: Break the tyrant's heart and back; uproot all his roots and branches from the base.
sentences.s469-s47: If I find one who sets up evil and does wicked deeds, how shall I go about searching for him?
sentences.s469-s48: I will hang alive the notorious one who has ruined the outcome from the very beginning.
sentences.s469-s37: So that there be no harm from thieves, remain cheerful‑hearted and useful.
sentences.s469-s38: From every city a fair worshipper — full of modesty, a wakeful‑hearted devotee.
sentences.s469-s39: who perhaps, by our golden musk (scent), may know how to worship our rite.
sentences.s469-s40: Such that whoever departs is not sullen; it should not be that he has done injustice to a captive.
sentences.s469-s29: My heart is bright yet full of pain; I will strive so that I am not torn away from his counsel.
sentences.s469-s30: Whoever comes to this court will find money, honor, a throne, and a hat (i.e. rank).
sentences.s469-s31: When he wishes to be in his own iwan (porch), may he not turn away, fleeing from his covenant.
sentences.s469-s32: They will find something the like of which they desire from the treasure; after that no one will see pain or suffering.
sentences.s469-s1: Someone came from Kerman toward Isfahan, to a place where guests from Iran were gathered.
sentences.s469-s2: A man with a retinue came up near the king's veiled-faced (modest) attendants.
sentences.s469-s3: Alexander sent them greetings; he recounted to them all the deeds of Dâra.
sentences.s469-s4: He said thus: that from the deaths of kings no justice comes — neither the enemy's heart nor the friend's is glad.
